title: Aspiration Pneumonia
oneLiner: Lung infection from inhaled oropharyngeal/gastric contents; common in dysphagia, reduced consciousness, elderly – high morbidity.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Large airway obstruction** (from solid particulate): acute stridor, severe hypoxia not correcting with O2, absent breath sounds one side → emergency bronchoscopy
- **Spontaneous tension pneumothorax** (from abscess rupture): tracheal deviation, hyperresonance, hypotension → immediate needle decompression
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms: cough (often productive with foul-smelling sputum), fever, dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, altered mental state in elderly
- Key exam findings: crackles in dependent lung zones (right lower lobe – most common if aspirated upright; posterior segments of upper lobes if supine), tachypnoea, hypoxia, signs of consolidation (dullness, bronchial breathing)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP)** – no clear aspiration event; typical lobar consolidation not in dependent segments
- **Pulmonary embolism** – pleuritic pain + hypoxia but no fever; raised D-dimer, Wells score
- **Pulmonary oedema** – bilateral crackles, orthopnoea, frothy sputum; no fever, CXR shows interstitial pattern
- **Foreign body aspiration** – sudden onset of cough/choking in a lucid patient; unilateral wheeze; needs bronchoscopy
- **Lung abscess** – often a complication of aspiration, not initial presentation; prolonged fever, weight loss, CT for cavitation
- **Splitter:** Foul-smelling sputum + infiltrate in dependent segment → aspiration pneumonia; CAP usually has non-foul sputum and non-dependent consolidation
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** pulse oximetry, capillary blood gas (ABG if SpO2 <92% or tachypnoeic), chest X-ray (CXR – erect PA or AP; if immobile consider supine with careful interpretation)
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, CRP, LFT (check for chronic liver disease – risk), blood cultures x2 sets (before antibiotics if possible), sputum culture (if expectorating), MRSA/MSSA screen if risk factors
- **Imaging:** CXR first-line – look for infiltrate in right lower lobe (most common) or posterior upper lobes; if CXR non-diagnostic but high suspicion, CT thorax
- **Special tests:** Swallowing assessment by speech and language therapy (SLT) – videofluoroscopy or fibreoptic endoscopic evaluation if feasible
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** Clinical suspicion + aspiration risk factors (dysphagia, poor dentition, reduced consciousness, alcohol misuse, seizure, GORD) + consistent CXR pattern + microbiology
- **Never-miss test:** **Chest X-ray** (to rule out pneumothorax, effusion, or large cavitation) AND **blood cultures** (to guide antibiotic sensitivity, avoid missing empyema/sepsis)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Airway, breathing, circulation – O2 to target SpO2 94-98% (88-92% if chronic hypercapnia)
  - IV access, IV fluids if hypotensive (0.9% saline or Hartmann's 500ml bolus, reassess)
  - Antibiotics as soon as possible (within 1 hour of recognition) – do not wait for cultures if septic
  - Consider NBM until SLT assessment
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics; step down to oral when afebrile and improving (usually 48-72h)
  - Chest physiotherapy (postural drainage, percussion if tolerated)
  - SLT swallowing assessment before any oral intake – if unsafe, consider nasogastric tube or parenteral nutrition
  - Review sedation and medications that reduce consciousness (opioids, benzodiazepines, antipsychotics)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete antibiotic course (total 5-10 days; longer if abscess/empyema)
  - Repeat CXR 6-8 weeks after discharge to exclude underlying neoplasm (especially in smokers >40 years)
  - Consider PEG if severe, irreversible dysphagia
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or balanced crystalloid at maintenance rate (e.g., 1L 8-hourly) – adjust for comorbid heart failure
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin (dose per local protocol, usually 40mg subcut daily) unless contraindicated (active bleed, coagulopathy, high bleeding risk)
  - **Diet / NBM:** NBM until SLT swallow assessment complete; if urgent nutrition needed, place NG tube
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 every 4 hours (if stable); hourly for first 6 hours if NEWS2 ≥5
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin/clavulanate) IV 1.2g 8-hourly** for 5-10 days (check local formulary for alternative if penicillin allergy)
- **Common alternatives (penicillin allergy or local resistance):**
  - **Doxycycline** 200mg PO/IV loading (first 24h) then 100mg daily + **metronidazole** 500mg IV/PO 8-hourly – anaerobic cover essential
  - **Clindamycin** 600mg IV/PO 8-hourly (covers most anaerobes and Gram-positive; avoid if risk of C. diff)
  - **Piperacillin/tazobactam** 4.5g IV 6-hourly (if severe sepsis, known aspiration of gastric contents, or hospital-acquired)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: avoid in penicillin hypersensitivity (immediate reaction); interacts with warfarin (↑ INR – check INR daily)
  - Doxycycline: avoid in children <12y and pregnancy; chelated by antacids, iron – separate by 2 hours
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: reduce dose interval if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g., 1.2g 12-hourly); [check local policy for exact dose]
  - Doxycycline: no dose adjustment (hepatically cleared); clindamycin: no adjustment in mild–moderate renal failure
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving co-amoxiclav without checking renal function** in elderly or CKD – risk of neurotoxicity (seizures) and accumulation; also **using metronidazole with warfarin** without INR monitoring (potentiate bleeding)
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Lung abscess (fever persisting >72h despite appropriate antibiotics, new cavitation on CXR/CT)
  - Empyema (pleuritic pain, swinging fever, pleural fluid on ultrasound – requires drainage)
  - Septic shock (persistent hypotension needing vasopressors, lactate >2 mmol/L)
  - Acute respiratory distress syndrome (severe hypoxia, bilateral infiltrates)
- **Chronic:**
  - Bronchiectasis (recurrent aspiration leads to airway damage and scarring)
  - Recurrent pneumonia (due to ongoing dysphagia or impaired cough)
  - Respiratory failure (type 2 if underlying COPD/dystrophies)
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality 20-30% in elderly hospitalised patients; higher if septic shock or multiresistant organisms (e.g., MRSA, Gram-negatives). Recovery often slower than CAP due to underlying aspiration risk. Poor functional outcome if dysphagia not addressed.
sections.Pearls (Positive Actions):
- **Call SLT before any oral food or fluid** – NBM until they assess; this prevents re-aspiration
- **Check for occult causes of aspiration** – review medications that depress consciousness (sedatives, opioids, antipsychotics) and consider ENT/neurology input if bulbar dysfunction suspected
- **Repeat CXR at 6-8 weeks post-discharge** (especially in smokers >40y) – aspiration pneumonia can mask bronchial carcinoma; if opacity not resolved, arrange CT thorax and bronchoscopy
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not start oral feeding without SLT assessment** – you will cause repeat aspiration and prolong illness
- **Do not use aminoglycosides as first-line** – poor lung penetration, nephrotoxic, and ineffective against anaerobes
- **Do not rely on sputum culture alone** – oropharyngeal contamination is common; if pleural fluid or blood cultures are negative but suspicion high, consider CT-guided aspiration of abscess/empyema
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening cough, new pleuritic chest pain, or recurrence of fever after initial improvement
  - Increasing shortness of breath or need for oxygen
  - Coughing up blood (haemoptysis) – may indicate abscess or TB/malignancy
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 2 weeks for clinical assessment; chest X-ray in 6-8 weeks (if not already arranged)
  - If dysphagia persists, refer to gastroenterology/ENT for further workup (manometry, endoscopy)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if any single parameter is 3 – e.g., SpO2, respiratory rate, BP)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L despite fluid resuscitation (consider septic shock)
  - GCS drop from baseline (beware airway compromise / meningitis / stroke)
  - Worsening hypoxia requiring FiO2 >50% or NIV / CPAP not improving – consider ARDS
  - New stridor or complete loss of breath sounds unilaterally – call anaesthetics / ENT for possible large airway obstruction

rawMarkdown:
## Aspiration Pneumonia
**One-liner:** Lung infection from inhaled oropharyngeal/gastric contents; common in dysphagia, reduced consciousness, elderly – high morbidity.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Large airway obstruction** (from solid particulate): acute stridor, severe hypoxia not correcting with O2, absent breath sounds one side → emergency bronchoscopy
- **Spontaneous tension pneumothorax** (from abscess rupture): tracheal deviation, hyperresonance, hypotension → immediate needle decompression

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms: cough (often productive with foul-smelling sputum), fever, dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, altered mental state in elderly
- Key exam findings: crackles in dependent lung zones (right lower lobe – most common if aspirated upright; posterior segments of upper lobes if supine), tachypnoea, hypoxia, signs of consolidation (dullness, bronchial breathing)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP)** – no clear aspiration event; typical lobar consolidation not in dependent segments
- **Pulmonary embolism** – pleuritic pain + hypoxia but no fever; raised D-dimer, Wells score
- **Pulmonary oedema** – bilateral crackles, orthopnoea, frothy sputum; no fever, CXR shows interstitial pattern
- **Foreign body aspiration** – sudden onset of cough/choking in a lucid patient; unilateral wheeze; needs bronchoscopy
- **Lung abscess** – often a complication of aspiration, not initial presentation; prolonged fever, weight loss, CT for cavitation
- **Splitter:** Foul-smelling sputum + infiltrate in dependent segment → aspiration pneumonia; CAP usually has non-foul sputum and non-dependent consolidation

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** pulse oximetry, capillary blood gas (ABG if SpO2 <92% or tachypnoeic), chest X-ray (CXR – erect PA or AP; if immobile consider supine with careful interpretation)
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, CRP, LFT (check for chronic liver disease – risk), blood cultures x2 sets (before antibiotics if possible), sputum culture (if expectorating), MRSA/MSSA screen if risk factors
- **Imaging:** CXR first-line – look for infiltrate in right lower lobe (most common) or posterior upper lobes; if CXR non-diagnostic but high suspicion, CT thorax
- **Special tests:** Swallowing assessment by speech and language therapy (SLT) – videofluoroscopy or fibreoptic endoscopic evaluation if feasible
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** Clinical suspicion + aspiration risk factors (dysphagia, poor dentition, reduced consciousness, alcohol misuse, seizure, GORD) + consistent CXR pattern + microbiology
- **Never-miss test:** **Chest X-ray** (to rule out pneumothorax, effusion, or large cavitation) AND **blood cultures** (to guide antibiotic sensitivity, avoid missing empyema/sepsis)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Airway, breathing, circulation – O2 to target SpO2 94-98% (88-92% if chronic hypercapnia)
  - IV access, IV fluids if hypotensive (0.9% saline or Hartmann's 500ml bolus, reassess)
  - Antibiotics as soon as possible (within 1 hour of recognition) – do not wait for cultures if septic
  - Consider NBM until SLT assessment
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics; step down to oral when afebrile and improving (usually 48-72h)
  - Chest physiotherapy (postural drainage, percussion if tolerated)
  - SLT swallowing assessment before any oral intake – if unsafe, consider nasogastric tube or parenteral nutrition
  - Review sedation and medications that reduce consciousness (opioids, benzodiazepines, antipsychotics)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete antibiotic course (total 5-10 days; longer if abscess/empyema)
  - Repeat CXR 6-8 weeks after discharge to exclude underlying neoplasm (especially in smokers >40 years)
  - Consider PEG if severe, irreversible dysphagia
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or balanced crystalloid at maintenance rate (e.g., 1L 8-hourly) – adjust for comorbid heart failure
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin (dose per local protocol, usually 40mg subcut daily) unless contraindicated (active bleed, coagulopathy, high bleeding risk)
  - **Diet / NBM:** NBM until SLT swallow assessment complete; if urgent nutrition needed, place NG tube
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 every 4 hours (if stable); hourly for first 6 hours if NEWS2 ≥5

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin/clavulanate) IV 1.2g 8-hourly** for 5-10 days (check local formulary for alternative if penicillin allergy)
- **Common alternatives (penicillin allergy or local resistance):**
  - **Doxycycline** 200mg PO/IV loading (first 24h) then 100mg daily + **metronidazole** 500mg IV/PO 8-hourly – anaerobic cover essential
  - **Clindamycin** 600mg IV/PO 8-hourly (covers most anaerobes and Gram-positive; avoid if risk of C. diff)
  - **Piperacillin/tazobactam** 4.5g IV 6-hourly (if severe sepsis, known aspiration of gastric contents, or hospital-acquired)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: avoid in penicillin hypersensitivity (immediate reaction); interacts with warfarin (↑ INR – check INR daily)
  - Doxycycline: avoid in children <12y and pregnancy; chelated by antacids, iron – separate by 2 hours
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: reduce dose interval if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g., 1.2g 12-hourly); [check local policy for exact dose]
  - Doxycycline: no dose adjustment (hepatically cleared); clindamycin: no adjustment in mild–moderate renal failure
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving co-amoxiclav without checking renal function** in elderly or CKD – risk of neurotoxicity (seizures) and accumulation; also **using metronidazole with warfarin** without INR monitoring (potentiate bleeding)

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Lung abscess (fever persisting >72h despite appropriate antibiotics, new cavitation on CXR/CT)
  - Empyema (pleuritic pain, swinging fever, pleural fluid on ultrasound – requires drainage)
  - Septic shock (persistent hypotension needing vasopressors, lactate >2 mmol/L)
  - Acute respiratory distress syndrome (severe hypoxia, bilateral infiltrates)
- **Chronic:**
  - Bronchiectasis (recurrent aspiration leads to airway damage and scarring)
  - Recurrent pneumonia (due to ongoing dysphagia or impaired cough)
  - Respiratory failure (type 2 if underlying COPD/dystrophies)

### Prognosis
- Mortality 20-30% in elderly hospitalised patients; higher if septic shock or multiresistant organisms (e.g., MRSA, Gram-negatives). Recovery often slower than CAP due to underlying aspiration risk. Poor functional outcome if dysphagia not addressed.

### Pearls (Positive Actions)
- **Call SLT before any oral food or fluid** – NBM until they assess; this prevents re-aspiration
- **Check for occult causes of aspiration** – review medications that depress consciousness (sedatives, opioids, antipsychotics) and consider ENT/neurology input if bulbar dysfunction suspected
- **Repeat CXR at 6-8 weeks post-discharge** (especially in smokers >40y) – aspiration pneumonia can mask bronchial carcinoma; if opacity not resolved, arrange CT thorax and bronchoscopy

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not start oral feeding without SLT assessment** – you will cause repeat aspiration and prolong illness
- **Do not use aminoglycosides as first-line** – poor lung penetration, nephrotoxic, and ineffective against anaerobes
- **Do not rely on sputum culture alone** – oropharyngeal contamination is common; if pleural fluid or blood cultures are negative but suspicion high, consider CT-guided aspiration of abscess/empyema

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening cough, new pleuritic chest pain, or recurrence of fever after initial improvement
  - Increasing shortness of breath or need for oxygen
  - Coughing up blood (haemoptysis) – may indicate abscess or TB/malignancy
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 2 weeks for clinical assessment; chest X-ray in 6-8 weeks (if not already arranged)
  - If dysphagia persists, refer to gastroenterology/ENT for further workup (manometry, endoscopy)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if any single parameter is 3 – e.g., SpO2, respiratory rate, BP)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L despite fluid resuscitation (consider septic shock)
  - GCS drop from baseline (beware airway compromise / meningitis / stroke)
  - Worsening hypoxia requiring FiO2 >50% or NIV / CPAP not improving – consider ARDS
  - New stridor or complete loss of breath sounds unilaterally – call anaesthetics / ENT for possible large airway obstruction
